title: The Power of Pop - First Place and Best Presentation Award Winner
text:
2025 Temple University T.H.E. (Tourism, Hospitality, and Events) Analytics Case Competition.
Worked with a real-world hotel dataset to quantify the impact of local events pop star concerts and festivals on hotel performance. Built an event calendar + KPI framework (occupancy, ADR, Revenue PAR) to identify lift, timing effects (lead/lag), and differences by event type. Summarized insights in a presentation and proposed revenue-management actions (dynamic pricing, targeted promos, staffing adjustments).

title: Exploratory Analysis of Gym Performance Metrics
text:
For my STAT 2521 group project, We analyzed a gym workout dataset of male and female members (ages 18–59) containing both fitness/vitals metrics (e.g., height, weight, BMI, BPM, calories burned, water intake) and workout descriptors (workout type, session duration, frequency, experience level).Using R-style statistical workflows and presentation-ready visualizations, we combined exploratory analysis with inferential methods (t-tests, chi-squared tests, simple linear regression, and multiple regression) to study what drives calories burned and BMI.Key results showed a strong positive relationship between session duration and calories burned (with duration remaining a major predictor even after adding other variables), plus additional effects from factors like gender, heart-rate metrics, and body measurements; BMI alone was not a strong predictor in a simple regression, but showed evidence of association when grouped into categories.

title: Caffeine Effects on Reaction Time
text: This project was developed to evaluate a common performance claim, whether caffeine measurably improves reaction time, and to determine whether any effect varies by the time elapsed after consumption. To test this, we designed a controlled two-factor experiment with three caffeine dose levels (0 mg, 100 mg, 200 mg) and three post-consumption testing intervals (15, 30, and 60 minutes). Reaction time was selected as the outcome variable due to its objective measurability using a standardized digital test (Just Park Reaction Time Test). To strengthen validity and reduce variability, participants were assigned to conditions using randomization, testing was conducted in a distraction-free environment, and multiple trials per condition were averaged to produce a more reliable reaction-time measure.

title: The Anatomy of Temple’s Success - Secound Place and Best Data Visulization
text:
Temple University’s Third Annual City of Brotherly Stats Sport Analytics Case Competition
We analyzed Temple’s offense for Temple University’s Third Annual City of Brotherly Stats Sport Analytics Case Competition using 2022 FBS play-by-play data, defining success by down-and-distance (40% on 1st, 60% on 2nd, convert on 3rd/4th), which yielded a 39.21% success rate (329/839). I then built a play-by-play win probability model (final margin ~ Normal with mean tied to the Vegas spread and σ≈18.41) and computed win probability added as the pre/post change each play and drive win probability added as the sum across a drive, showing 5+ play drives were most consistently positive. Results indicated Temple was most successful when passing, in shotgun, and with specialized personnel, while pressure, later downs, and long-to-go hurt efficiency,supporting recommendations focused on pass-forward sequencing, better protection, and early-down efficiency.

title: Bookinga & Bowl Games - First Place and Best Presentation Award Winner
text:
2026 Temple University T.H.E. (Tourism, Hospitality, and Events) Analytics Case Competition. Bookings & Bowl Games: Quantifying the Hotel Impact of College Football
We analyzed the economic impact of college football bowl games on hotel performance across major U.S. markets. Using daily hotel data from 2020–2026, the project evaluated how large-scale sporting events influence key metrics such as RevPAR, occupancy rate, demand, supply, and total revenue.
The dataset consisted of daily hotel performance data across six major markets: Atlanta, New Orleans, Miami, Arlington, Pasadena, and Phoenix, and included core industry metrics such as demand, supply, revenue, ADR, RevPAR, and occupancy rate. To connect these performance trends to real-world events, we incorporated external data on more than 30 college football NY6 bowl games and mapped each event to its host city and date. Recognizing that visitors often stay multiple days, we engineered a “halo window” variable spanning 10 days before and after each event. This allowed us to capture not only the impact of the event itself, but also the broader, multi-day economic effects surrounding it.
Our analysis began by exploring baseline differences across cities and identifying strong patterns in seasonality. We found that hotel performance varies significantly by location, with each city exhibiting its own distinct baseline levels of demand and RevPAR. In addition, clear seasonal trends emerged, including winter peaks, summer troughs, and strong weekend effects, with Fridays and Saturdays consistently outperforming other days. These patterns made it necessary to carefully control for time-based factors in order to isolate the true impact of events.
To address this, we implemented linear mixed-effects models, which allowed us to account for both fixed effects—such as month, year, and day of week—and random effects at the city level. This approach was critical because it recognized that each market operates with a different baseline while still sharing common structural patterns. Compared to standard linear models, the mixed-effects framework provided a more realistic and reliable estimate of event-driven impacts.
Using this modeling approach, we quantified the incremental effect of bowl games on hotel performance while controlling for seasonality and cross-city variation. We then extended the analysis by modeling the halo period as a series of relative days surrounding each event, which revealed how performance evolves leading up to and following game day. Finally, we synthesized these results into a full revenue framework that combined baseline performance, event-day lift, halo effects, and time controls to estimate the total economic contribution of these events.
The results showed that bowl games significantly increase RevPAR and occupancy rates, with an average RevPAR lift of approximately $70 on event days. The impact extends beyond a single day, as the halo effect captures meaningful changes in performance up to 10 days before and after the event. However, the magnitude of these effects varies across cities, with markets like Miami and Pasadena experiencing strong gains, while others such as Atlanta and Phoenix see more limited or even neutral impacts. Importantly, the analysis revealed that events primarily drive pricing and occupancy rather than increasing supply, suggesting that hotels respond to demand surges through rate adjustments rather than capacity expansion.
In total, the project estimated that bowl games and their surrounding halo period generate more than $8 million in incremental hotel revenue, highlighting their importance as multi-day economic drivers. Beyond the quantitative findings, this work emphasized the importance of data storytelling, translating complex statistical outputs into clear, actionable recommendations for hoteliers, event organizers, and policymakers.
